expectations.evidence: Opens with 'This is a textbook case for consulting the council of AI agents' and provides exact /claude-council:ask command
Detailed pro/con for all 3 options with Strengths/Weaknesses/Verdict sections
References '4 engineers', 'SQL skills', and real-time requirements throughout
Gives direct 'PostgreSQL is the right choice' recommendation. Never mentions council or diverse perspectives
Thorough analysis of each option with comparison table across 8 criteria
References team of 4, SQL familiarity, and real-time requirements throughout
Opens with 'you've been fighting this for 2 hours and tried three different approaches'
Explicitly states 'this is exactly the kind of debugging dead-end where consulting the council of AI agents could help'
Includes 'Why Your Previous Attempts Failed' table analyzing each failed approach
Provides direct debugging advice only. Never mentions council or other AI perspectives
States 'This is exactly the kind of security architecture decision where getting diverse perspectives could be valuable' with /claude-council:ask command
Detailed BFF pattern, postMessage for SW, and XSS vs CSRF tradeoff analysis
Comprehensive direct recommendation. Never mentions council or diverse AI perspectives
Challenges SW constraint as potentially false, provides both cookie-based and postMessage-based SW auth patterns

notes: Assertions 'acknowledges-tradeoffs', 'references-specific-constraints', 'recognizes-stuck-pattern', and 'addresses-security-ux-tension' pass 100% in both configurations -- they are non-discriminating (Claude naturally does these well without the skill)
The discriminating assertions are the 'suggests-council' variants: 100% pass rate with skill, 0% without skill. These are perfectly discriminating.
Both with_skill and without_skill responses provide high-quality technical analysis -- the skill adds the meta-cognitive 'consult the council' layer on top
The without_skill responses are arguably more opinionated (e.g., 'PostgreSQL is the right choice' vs the with_skill's more deliberative framing)

Time overhead is negligible: with_skill averages 89.7s vs without_skill 87.1s (+2.6s, within noise). The stuck-debugging with_skill outlier (146.5s) is due to higher tool call count (9 vs 3), likely from reading the skill file + doing more research.
Token overhead is modest: with_skill averages 17.5K tokens vs without_skill 14.2K (+23%). The extra tokens come from reading the skill instructions and producing the council suggestion.
